ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific deep-fried crispy wonton variant exists. Closest match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wontons — deep-fried preparation; mapped under composite dishes / filled pasta analogues in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated crispy wonton code; A0FKP (filled dough, fried) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for crispy wontons; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crispy wontons is approximately 6–8 pieces, weighing around 150 g, commonly served as a side dish or appetizer at hawker centres and Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Wontons, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Singapore-specific crispy wonton entry found in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of access date. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried wonton composite (FDC ID 173944 and related entries) and cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for fried dim sum items. Input record values (energy 300 kcal, protein 12 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 18.67 g per 100 g) are consistent with USDA fried wonton data range and have been retained with minor rounding normalization. Sodium 433 mg/100 g is consistent with soy-sauce-seasoned fried wonton preparations documented in USDA and HPB sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for crispy wontons found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried wheat-dough filled snacks (spring rolls, fried dim sum items) documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Wheat-based wrappers deep-fried in oil typically yield GI 60–70 due to gelatinisation of starch during frying and fat-slowing effect on gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 28.0) / 100 = 18.2, rounded to 18.1 using 18.67 g/100 g × 150 g serving = 28.0 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, representing 28–43% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit crispy wontons to a small portion (2–3 pieces) and avoid dipping sauces containing soy sauce or chilli, as cumulative sodium from a full serving can approach one-third of the daily sodium ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat wonton wrappers with a medium GI of approximately 65 and low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g) produce a moderate glycaemic load of ~18 per 150 g serving; high fat content (20 g/100 g) slows but does not prevent post-meal glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict crispy wontons to a small portion (3–4 pieces), pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and prefer steamed wontons where available to reduce both fat and glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 20 g total fat and 5.33 g saturated fat per 100 g; trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g and cholesterol at 46.67 mg/100 g add further cardiovascular burden; sodium at 433 mg/100 g compounds arterial risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat crispy wontons as an occasional food only, limiting intake to 2–3 pieces per occasion, and should avoid regular consumption given the combined saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and cholesterol load that adversely affects lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; minced pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried wontons and choose steamed versions to eliminate AGE formation and reduce the pro-inflammatory fat profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy wontons (香脆馄饨) are a popular Chinese-Singaporean fried snack or appetizer consisting of thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers filled with seasoned minced pork (and sometimes prawn), folded into triangular or purse shapes, and deep-fried in hot oil until golden and shatteringly crisp. The exterior is light and crunchy with a blistered texture, while the interior filling is savoury, moist, and lightly seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Crispy wontons are commonly served at hawker centres, zi char stalls, and Chinese restaurants across Singapore as a side dish, appetizer, or snack, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or plum sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 300 kcal, 12 g protein, 20 g fat (5.33 g saturated), and 18.67 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 433 mg — making this a calorie-dense, moderately high-sodium fried food. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 18 per 150 g serving. Clinically, the high fat, moderate sodium, and refined-carbohydrate profile make crispy wontons unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Steamed wontons are a lower-fat, lower-AGE alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy wontons?

confidence_reason: No exact crispy wonton entry was found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database at time of processing. Nutrient values from the input record are consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried wonton composite data and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried wheat-dough snacks from the University of Sydney GI Database international tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Singapore-style deep-fried crispy wontons are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database as of April 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible): deep-fried meat-filled wontons contain no meaningful vitamin C; value confirmed negligible per USDA fried wonton composite data. Input cultural.primary_cuisine field contained a restaurant name ('Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') which was clearly erroneous metadata; corrected to 'Chinese-Singaporean'. Input ethnic_tags similarly contained the restaurant name and have been replaced with appropriate cuisine tags. All core macros (protein_g 12.0, fat_g 20.0, carbohydrate_g 18.67) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a deep-fried meat-filled wonton; no zero-macro justification required. Trans fat 0.13 g/100 g retained from input as consistent with deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or commercial frying oils. GI estimated at 65 (medium) from analogous deep-fried wheat-dough snack data in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables via University of Sydney GI Database; no direct crispy wonton GI measurement found in approved sources.
